summary: This grab-bag post covers three topics: Spolsky launching an 'Ask Joel' Q&A forum inspired by Philip Greenspun, a detailed argument about why Microsoft needs to provide a linker for .NET so developers can ship standalone executables, and brief recommendations of blogs and Why's Poignant Guide to Ruby. The linker section is the meat of the post, where Spolsky responds to a Microsoft program manager who dismissed his request. He argues that the security-patching concern is solvable with jump tables and that Microsoft's refusal mirrors Ashton-Tate's fatal mistake of not giving dBase developers a linker, which let FoxPro steal the market. Spolsky concludes that Microsoft has grown so insular they've lost touch with what ISVs actually need.
key_points: Microsoft dismissed Spolsky's request for a .NET linker, citing security patching and working set size concerns, but Spolsky argues both are solvable technical problems
The lack of standalone executables has been the #1 weakness of VB and .NET according to ISVs Spolsky has talked to, echoing a 13-year-old complaint from Visual Basic 1.0 days
Historical precedent: FoxPro beat dBase largely because it could compile standalone executables, and Ashton-Tate's refusal to provide a linker contributed to their downfall
Large companies like Microsoft develop a reality-distortion field where employees on a 45-building campus lose track of what the outside world needs
Microsoft's culture tends to treat customer requests as intellectual challenges to be debated and deflected rather than problems to be solved
money_quotes: There's a really strong tendency in that culture to treat customer requests as fun intellectual challenges to be fended off like exercises in debating class.
I'm forced to conclude that Microsoft has grown so large they are living in a reality-distortion field.
It's not unusual for someone working on a campus with 45 buildings all full of Microsoft employees to lose track of what the outside world is thinking and doing.
In the late 80s FoxPro clobbered dBase in the market mainly on the strength of the fact that it compiled standalone executables.
Let me pick the tradeoff I want to make between ease of installation and working set size.
My opinion is far too shallow and insipid to justify such an effort.
key_insight: Large platform companies develop institutional blindness to developer needs, treating legitimate customer requests as debating exercises rather than competitive threats — a pattern that historically lets nimbler competitors steal the market.
